title: Direction Sense

mental_model.intuition: You are drawing a map from a fixed origin.
mental_model.description: Direction problems test your ability to maintain a constant frame of reference. By plotting lines proportionally from a starting origin point and rotating your perspective for turns, complex paths reduce to simple geometry.

method: Draw a standard compass cross on your scratchpad (North up, South down, East right, West left).
Start every problem by marking a clear central origin point (the starting point).
Trace the path step-by-step. Draw lines that are roughly proportional to the given distances (e.g., a 10m line should look twice as long as a 5m line).
For left/right turns, imagine yourself standing on the paper facing the current direction. A 'Right' turn is always 90 degrees clockwise; a 'Left' turn is 90 degrees counter-clockwise.

To find the final distance from the start, draw a straight line connecting the end point to the origin. If it's diagonal, drop a perpendicular line to form a right triangle and use Pythagoras' theorem (a^2 + b^2 = c^2).

worked_example.problem: A person walks 4 km East, then turns Left and walks 3 km. How far and in which direction is they from the starting point?
steps: Mark the origin point. Draw a line extending to the right (East) and label it '4'.
Facing East, a 'Left' turn means turning counter-clockwise, which is North (up).
Draw a line pointing up from the end of the 4km line, and label it '3'.
Connect the final point back to the origin. This forms a right-angled triangle with base 4 and height 3.
Determine direction: The end point is above and to the right of the origin, which is North-East.

common_mistakes.title: Drawing disproportionate lines
common_mistakes.explanation: If your 10m line is drawn shorter than your 5m line, you will misjudge the final position relative to the starting point and guess the wrong direction.

common_mistakes.title: Confusing left/right when facing South
common_mistakes.explanation: When facing South (down), a 'Right' turn goes West (left on the paper). Not reversing your perspective causes mirrored mistakes.

recall_questions.q: What rule of thumb prevents direction errors on left/right turns?
recall_questions.answer: A Right turn is always 90 degrees clockwise; a Left turn is always 90 degrees counter-clockwise.

recall_questions.q: Why is it important to draw line segments proportionally?
recall_questions.answer: Because intersecting lines and final positions relative to the start depend on the actual lengths; bad proportions lead to wrong direction guesses.

recall_questions.q: How do you calculate the shortest distance if the endpoint is diagonal from the start?
recall_questions.answer: Form a right-angled triangle using vertical and horizontal offsets and use Pythagoras' theorem.

oa_questions.question: A man walks 5 km South, turns right and walks 3 km, then turns right again and walks 5 km. In which direction is he from the starting point?
oa_questions.approach: He goes South (down) 5. Turning right (clockwise) means going West (left) 3. Turning right again (clockwise) means going North (up) 5. He is now exactly 3 km West of his starting point.

oa_questions.question: Starting from point X, a girl walks 15m West, turns left and walks 20m, then turns left again and walks 15m. How far is she from X?
oa_questions.approach: West 15, left (South) 20, left (East) 15. The final East path cancels out the initial West path. She is exactly 20m South of point X.
